howItWorks.title:
How HIV
Self-Testing Works
howItWorks.subtitle: Understand the science, accuracy, and window period behind the ORAQuick oral test.
howItWorks.s1title: What is the ORAQuick HIV Self-Test?
howItWorks.s1content: ORAQuick is an FDA-approved rapid HIV self-test that detects HIV-1 and HIV-2 antibodies using a simple gum swab — no blood, no needles. You collect a saliva sample from your gums and get a result in 20–40 minutes in complete privacy.
howItWorks.s2content: ORAQuick detects antibodies your immune system produces in response to HIV infection. It does not detect the virus directly, which is why there is a window period between infection and a reliable result.
howItWorks.s2note: ORAQuick does NOT detect the p24 antigen or viral RNA — it is an antibody-only test.
howItWorks.s3note: A reactive (positive) self-test is NOT a diagnosis. Confirmatory testing at a health facility is required.
howItWorks.s3subNote: Real-world home-use sensitivity was 91.7% — lower than lab conditions, underscoring the importance of following instructions carefully.
howItWorks.s4content: After HIV infection, the body takes time to produce detectable antibody levels. Testing too soon may give a false negative result.
howItWorks.s4timelineNote: Test at 3 months (90 days) after possible exposure for the most reliable result.

howToUse.title:
How to Use
ORAQuick
howToUse.subtitle: Gum swab · Results in 20–40 min · Follow all 5 steps carefully.
howToUse.importantNote: A reactive ORAQuick result is not a final HIV diagnosis. Visit a health facility for a confirmatory blood test.

steps.s1desc: Do NOT eat, drink, smoke, chew gum, or use any oral hygiene products for at least 30 minutes before testing.
steps.s1c1: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water
steps.s1c2: Check the expiry date printed on the foil pouch
steps.s1c3: Lay out all components: test device, developer vial, and swab
steps.s1warn: Testing within 30 minutes of eating or drinking can affect accuracy.
steps.s2desc: Hold the white handle end. Do NOT touch the flat swab pad with your fingers.
steps.s2c1: Swipe the pad once along your UPPER gum line — from back to front (cheek side, not teeth)
steps.s2c2: Using the same swab, swipe your LOWER gum line once — from back to front
steps.s2c3: One full swipe per gum line is sufficient
steps.s2note: Do NOT swab your teeth, tongue, or inner cheeks.
steps.s3desc: Remove the purple cap from the developer vial. Insert the swab paddle-end first, straight down into the vial.
steps.s3c1: Stand the vial upright — use the included stand or lean it against a wall
steps.s3c2: Do NOT lay the vial on its side after inserting the swab
steps.s3c3: The developer fluid will travel up the test strip on its own
steps.s3note: Do not stir, shake, or move the vial after inserting the swab.
steps.s4desc: Set a timer for 20 minutes and leave the vial completely undisturbed on a flat surface.
steps.s4c1: Do not move, tilt, or shake the vial while waiting
steps.s4c2: Do NOT read results before 20 minutes — the reaction is still developing
steps.s4c3: Do NOT read results after 40 minutes — result will no longer be valid
steps.s4warn: Reading outside the 20–40 minute window gives an unreliable result.
steps.s5desc: Look at the result window on the test device. Check for lines next to "C" (Control) and "T" (Test).
steps.r1detail: HIV not detected at this time.
steps.r2detail: Even a faint T line is reactive. Seek confirmatory testing.
steps.r3detail: Test did not work. Retest with a new kit.

timer.title: Guided Test Timer
timer.subtitle: Step-by-step with built-in 20-min timer
timer.developing: Developing... do not touch the vial
timer.windowOpen: Read window open — you can read now ✓
timer.expired: Result expired — retest with a new kit
timer.waitWarning: Wait until 20:00 before reading your result. Do not touch the vial.
timer.doneSub: Head to results to understand your result

results.title: Understanding Results
results.subtitle: ORAQuick HIV Self-Test
results.privacyNote: Results are not stored unless you give permission.

negative.title: Negative Result
negative.subtitle: No HIV antibodies were detected in your sample at this time.
negative.meaning: Your test did not detect HIV antibodies. If you had a potential exposure in the last 3 months, test again after the window period ends.
negative.step1: If exposed in the last 3 months, retest after the 90-day window period
negative.step2: Continue using protection (condoms, PrEP) to stay HIV-negative
negative.step3: Regular testing every 3–6 months if you are at ongoing risk

positive.title: Reactive Result
positive.subtitle: A reactive result was detected. This must be confirmed by a healthcare professional.
positive.meaning: The ORAQuick test detected HIV antibodies in your sample. This is NOT a final diagnosis — a reactive self-test result must be confirmed with a blood test at a health facility.
positive.step1: Visit a health facility as soon as possible for a confirmatory blood test
positive.step2: Speak with a counsellor — you don't have to face this alone
positive.step3: With early treatment (ART), people with HIV live full, healthy lives

invalid.title: Invalid Result
invalid.subtitle: The test did not produce a readable result. You need to retest.
invalid.meaning: The control line (C) did not appear, meaning the test did not run correctly. This can happen if instructions were not followed exactly or if the kit was expired.
invalid.step1: Re-read the instructions carefully before retesting — common errors include swabbing too briefly or disturbing the vial
invalid.step2: Check the expiry date on the kit pouch before retesting
invalid.step3: If you get another invalid result, visit a health facility for testing

chat.title: Health Assistant
chat.subtitle: Anonymous · Always private
chat.heroSubtitle: Your identity is never stored. Ask our health assistant any question about HIV, STIs, or self-testing.
chat.disclaimer: Not a substitute for medical advice. Results may vary.
chat.initialMessage: Hello 👋 I'm your anonymous health assistant. You can ask me anything about HIV self-testing, results, or next steps. Your identity is never stored.
chat.fallback: That's a great question. For personalized medical advice, please consult a healthcare professional or visit your nearest health center. I can share general information about HIV, STIs, and self-testing — what would you like to know?

quickReplies.q1: What does a positive result mean?
quickReplies.q2: Where can I find a clinic nearby?

responses.q1: A positive self-test result is NOT a confirmed diagnosis — it means HIV antibodies were detected. You need a confirmatory test at a clinic or hospital. Stay calm: early treatment works very well. Would you like help finding a nearby testing center?
responses.q2: You can find pharmacies with ORAQuick kits in the Find Test Kits section of this app. For full confirmatory testing, visit your nearest public health center — most offer free, confidential HIV testing.
responses.q3: PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis) is emergency HIV medication taken within 72 hours of a potential exposure. It must be started as soon as possible — ideally within 2 hours. Visit an emergency room or clinic immediately if you think you've been exposed.
responses.q4: ORAQuick has 99.3% sensitivity (detects true positives) and 99.8% specificity (avoids false positives) in lab settings. Home use sensitivity is around 91.7%. A negative result during the window period should be confirmed with a retest at 90 days.
responses.q5: The window period is the time after infection when antibodies haven't built up enough to be detected — typically 18–90 days for ORAQuick. If you tested negative but had a recent potential exposure, retest at 90 days to be sure.

community.disclaimer: No account needed. Your identity is never stored.

sexualHealth.title: Know Your Sexual Health
sexualHealth.subtitle: STI information
sexualHealth.description: Clear, stigma-free information on common STIs — symptoms, prevention, and treatment.
sexualHealth.getTestedDesc: Many STIs are silent. Annual testing is recommended for sexually active individuals.

facts.f1desc: Regular testing is the only way to know your status — even if you feel healthy.
facts.f2desc: Condoms, vaccines (HPV, HepB), and PrEP are highly effective when used correctly.
facts.f3desc: Most bacterial STIs are curable. Viral STIs are manageable. Don't delay testing.

chlamydia.name: Chlamydia
chlamydia.desc: Often no symptoms. Easily treated with antibiotics.
chlamydia.treatment: Single-dose or 7-day antibiotic course (azithromycin or doxycycline)

gonorrhea.name: Gonorrhea
gonorrhea.desc: Can affect multiple areas. Antibiotic-resistant strains exist.

syphilis.name: Syphilis
syphilis.desc: 3 stages. Curable if caught early. Serious if untreated.
syphilis.transmission: Direct contact with sores during sex
syphilis.treatment: Penicillin injection (highly effective in early stages)

hepatitisB.name: Hepatitis B
hepatitisB.transmission: Unprotected sex, sharing needles, mother to child
hepatitisB.treatment: Vaccine available (3-dose series). Antivirals for chronic infection

hpv.name: HPV
hpv.desc: Very common. Vaccine available. Some strains are high-risk.
hpv.treatment: Vaccine available. Warts can be treated. Cervical changes monitored
hpv.testing: Pap smear / HPV DNA test (for women)

herpes.name: Herpes
herpes.desc: Lifelong but manageable. Antivirals reduce outbreaks.
herpes.transmission: Skin-to-skin contact, even without visible sores
herpes.treatment: No cure, but antivirals (acyclovir, valacyclovir) reduce outbreaks
herpes.testing: Visual exam, swab of active sore, or blood test
